markets.question: Will the Fed Cut–Pause–Pause in the next three decisions (Apr–Jun–Jul)?
markets.description:
The FED interest rates are defined in this market by the upper bound of the target federal funds rate. The decisions on the target federal funds rate are made by the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meetings.

This market will resolve according to the decisions made by the next three Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meetings: April 28-29; June 16-17; and July 28-29.

A qualifying cut occurs when the new upper bound of the target federal funds rate is lower compared to the level it was prior to the respective meeting.

A qualifying hike occurs when the new upper bound of the target federal funds rate is higher compared to the level it was prior to the respective meeting.

A qualifying pause occurs when the new upper bound of the target federal funds rate is equal to the level it was prior to the respective meeting.

If the Fed publishes a different combination than any listed, this market will resolve to "Other". Any rate hike will be encompassed by "Other".

Emergency rate cuts outside the regularly scheduled meetings will not be considered.

The resolution source for this market is the FOMC’s statement after its meetings:
https://www.federalreserve.gov/monetarypolicy/fomccalendars.htm

The level and change of the target federal funds rate is also published at the official website of the Federal Reserve:
https://www.federalreserve.gov/monetarypolicy/openmarket.htm

events.description:

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus on Polymarket prices an 78.5% implied probability for the Federal Reserve to pause interest rates at the April 28-29, June 16-17, and July 28-29 FOMC meetings, reflecting sticky inflation and a resilient labor market amid geopolitical oil shocks. March 2026 CPI surged 3.3% year-over-year—driven by energy prices amid Iran tensions—with core at 2.6%, while nonfarm payrolls added a stronger-than-expected 178,000 jobs and unemployment held at 4.3%. The Fed maintained its 3.50%-3.75% target range at the March 17-18 meeting, signaling one cut later in 2026. CME FedWatch aligns, showing >98% April hold odds and elevated pause probabilities through summer, with April CPI (due May 12) as the next key catalyst.

markets.question: Stefan Dostanic vs. Federico Agustin Gomez: Total Sets O/U 2.5
markets.description:
This market refers to the tennis match between Stefan Dostanic and Federico Agustin Gomez in the Savannah, originally scheduled for April 20, 2026 at 10:00AM ET.
This market will resolve to "Over" if the total number of sets completed equals or exceeds 3. Otherwise, it will resolve to "Under."
A super tie breaker is considered as one (1) set for total set markets.
If the match begins but is not completed, this market will resolve 50-50. If the match is canceled before play begins or delayed beyond 7 days from the scheduled date without a result, this market will also resolve 50-50.
Resolution will be based on official Challenger statistics.
markets.groupItemTitle: Savannah: Stefan Dostanic vs Federico Agustin Gomez Total Sets: O/U 2.5

events.title: Savannah: Stefan Dostanic vs Federico Agustin Gomez
events.description:
This market refers to the tennis match between Stefan Dostanic and Federico Agustin Gomez in the Savannah, originally scheduled for April 20, 2026 at 10:00AM ET.

This market will resolve to 'Stefan Dostanic' if Stefan Dostanic advances against Federico Agustin Gomez.

This market will resolve to 'Federico Agustin Gomez' if Federico Agustin Gomez advances against Stefan Dostanic.

If the match is canceled (not played at all), ends in a tie, or is delayed beyond 7 days from the scheduled date without a winner determined, this market will resolve to 50-50.

If the match begins but is not completed, and one player advances due to the opponent's retirement, default, or disqualification, this market will resolve to the player who advances.

If the match ends in a walkover (player withdraws before the start and the other advances automatically), this market will resolve to 50-50.

The primary resolution source will be official information from the ATP Tour. A consensus of credible reporting may also be used.

eventMetadata.context_description: Federico Agustin Gomez, ranked No. 167, holds the edge over American wildcard Stefan Dostanic (No. 268) in their first-round Savannah Challenger clash on outdoor green clay, reflecting trader consensus on Gomez's superior Challenger experience and clay-court affinity as an Argentine. Both arrive off round-of-32 losses at last week's Tallahassee clay event—Dostanic to Daniil Glinka, Gomez retiring injured against Michael Mmoh—following Dostanic's Sarasota final retirement and Gomez's quarterfinal run there. No head-to-head history or reported injuries; Dostanic benefits from home-crowd support at the Landings Club, while weather and fatigue from recent heavy clay slates loom as variables in this closely contested matchup.

markets.question: Will 4 Fed rate cuts happen in 2026?
markets.description:
This market will resolve according to the exact amount of cuts of 25 basis points in 2026 by the Fed (including any cuts made during the December meeting).

Emergency rate cuts outside of scheduled FOMC meetings will also count toward the total number of cuts in 2026. This market will remain open until December 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET, to account for any such emergency actions.

For example, if the Fed cuts rates by 50 bps after a meeting, it would be considered 2 cuts (of 25 bps each).

This market will resolve early to "No" if the specified number of cuts becomes impossible — i.e., if more cuts have already occurred than the strike in question.

Note that cuts between 1–24 bps (inclusive) will also be considered 1 rate cut.

The resolution source for this market will be FOMC statements after meetings scheduled in 2026 according to the official calendar: https://www.federalreserve.gov/monetarypolicy/fomccalendars.htm. The level and change of the target federal funds rate is also published at the official website of the Federal Reserve at https://www.federalreserve.gov/monetarypolicy/openmarket.htm.

events.title: How many Fed rate cuts in 2026?
events.description:
This market will resolve according to the exact amount of cuts of 25 basis points in 2026 by the Fed (including any cuts made during the December meeting).

Emergency rate cuts outside of scheduled FOMC meetings will also count toward the total number of cuts in 2026. This market will remain open until December 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET, to account for any such emergency actions.

For example, if the Fed cuts rates by 50 bps after a meeting, it would be considered 2 cuts (of 25 bps each).

This market will resolve early to "No" if the specified number of cuts becomes impossible — i.e., if more cuts have already occurred than the strike in question.

Note that cuts between 1–24 bps (inclusive) will also be considered 1 rate cut.

The resolution source for this market will be FOMC statements after meetings scheduled in 2026 according to the official calendar: https://www.federalreserve.gov/monetarypolicy/fomccalendars.htm. The level and change of the target federal funds rate is also published at the official website of the Federal Reserve at https://www.federalreserve.gov/monetarypolicy/openmarket.htm.

eventMetadata.context_description: Polymarket traders remain closely divided on 2026 Fed rate cuts, with 34% implying zero basis points of easing versus 29% for one 25 bps reduction, reflecting resilient U.S. labor markets and sticky inflation amid the federal funds rate steady at 3.50%-3.75%. The March 18 FOMC dot plot median projects a 3.4% year-end rate, signaling one cut, but February core PCE inflation held at 3% and March CPI showed energy prices up 12.5% year-over-year, tempering dovish bets. Stable unemployment near 4.4% and low job gains further support caution. Key swing factors include April 28-29 FOMC guidance and upcoming PCE data; sustained disinflation could favor cuts, while persistent pressures bolster the no-cut consensus.

markets.question: Will Trump end Department of Education before 2027?
markets.description:
This market will resolve to "Yes" if the US Department of Education ceases operations entirely, including the termination of all federal educational programs managed by the department, by December 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET. Otherwise, this market will resolve to "No".

If the US Department of Education is merged with another agency, resulting in a consolidated department with a shared administrative structure which is no longer titled the Department of Education it will count as a "Yes" resolution.

If it becomes impossible for Trump to sign legislation/perform executive actions (e.g. he resigns), this market will resolve to "No".

The primary resolution source for this market will be official information from the US government, however a consensus of credible reporting will also be used.

events.title: Will Trump end Department of Education before 2027?
events.description:
This market will resolve to "Yes" if the US Department of Education ceases operations entirely, including the termination of all federal educational programs managed by the department, by December 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET. Otherwise, this market will resolve to "No".

If the US Department of Education is merged with another agency, resulting in a consolidated department with a shared administrative structure which is no longer titled the Department of Education it will count as a "Yes" resolution.

If it becomes impossible for Trump to sign legislation/perform executive actions (e.g. he resigns), this market will resolve to "No".

The primary resolution source for this market will be official information from the US government, however a consensus of credible reporting will also be used.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus reflects near-certainty at 96.7% that President Trump will not fully eliminate the Department of Education before 2027, as abolishing the cabinet agency requires congressional legislation amid stalled progress on H.R. 899, introduced in January 2025 to terminate it by December 31, 2026. Executive orders in March 2025 and November 2025 initiated restructuring by shifting grants and functions to states and other agencies under Secretary Linda McMahon, but Congress rejected defunding requests in the February 2026 spending law, preserving appropriations. With eight months remaining before the deadline and midterm elections looming, key Republican holdouts favor reform over outright elimination, facing filibuster hurdles without 60 Senate votes or successful reconciliation. Late-breaking scandals, unified GOP supermajorities post-midterms, or expedited budget maneuvers could marginally shift odds, though structural barriers remain formidable.
